text: De Testamento by Heinrich Bullinger, published in 1534, is a very complete and multifaceted biblical and theological work on the covenant, embracing a wide range of topics, among them justification by faith, divine accommodation, the role of the Holy Spirit, the seed of Abraham and ethics. After outlining the relevance of the covenant for the whole of Scripture, Bullinger affirms the covenant as a thread of unity throughout the entire Bible, holding the Old and New Testaments together as a unified whole. This research, by investigating Bullinger’s vocabulary and usage, shows that while the reformer seemingly employs the terms foedus, testamentum and pactum as synonyms, he exhibits particular caution when referring to the covenant as a pactum, wishing to avoid any connection with the current scholastic thought of the pactum-theology. Furthermore, the inclusion of the covenant with Adam in later editions provides a comprehensive perspective of Bullinger’s salvation history. De Testamento shows Bullinger to be an especially thorough biblical theologian of the covenant, while his independent of Zwingli and in a spirited attack on the church’s clergy to challenge the elect to a life of integrity before their sovereign God.
explanation: The passage contains nuanced historical detail and scholarly phrasing with slight syntactic irregularities, which are more typical of human-authored academic writing than generic AI-generated text.
